context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m kinda lost on this NGOs/social movements reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through this! Before we dive in, let's start with what you already know. In your own words, what do you think an NGO actually does in a development context? Don't worry about being precise — just tell me your initial understanding.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would be doing too much of the work for them. Instead, I should acknowledge their uncertainty but redirect them to engage with the material themselves using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking them what they already know or think about NGOs as development agents, to get them to start generating their own ideas rather than passively receiving information.

exchanges.student: idk like they help people somehow, maybe with poverty or services, but i’m not really sure rn. ngl the whole civil society part is confusing me too.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point. Let's build on it. Think about your everyday life — when something needs to get done in a community, who are the actors involved? It doesn't have to be just government or just individuals. Who else shows up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a very vague understanding but is engaging honestly. I should acknowledge this starting point and use a simple question to help them build up their understanding of civil society organically, since that seems to be the sticking point. I'll ask them to think about what actors exist between the government and the individual, which is a classic framing for civil society.

exchanges.student: umm govt, maybe charities, random local groups? tbh that’s why I’m confused, it all kinda blends together for me.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think — charities, local groups, associations that aren't government and aren't private businesses... that's essentially what civil society is. Now here's a question: what do you think is the difference between an NGO and a social movement? How would you tell them apart?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually very close to defining civil society without realizing it. I should point this out and help them see that their intuition is correct, then push them to think about what distinguishes NGOs from social movements within that space, since the prompt asks about both.

exchanges.student: maybe NGOs are more official? and social movements are like protests? but can u just explain the difference from scratch bc i keep mixing everything up.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about NGOs being 'more official' is worth exploring. What do you think makes something 'official' in this context — like, what structures or features would an NGO have that a social movement might not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the difference from scratch, which would be doing the work for them. However, their intuition about NGOs being 'more official' and social movements being about 'protests' is actually a decent starting point. I should validate what's useful in their thinking and push them to develop it further with a targeted question rather than explaining it myself.

exchanges.student: idk, like funding maybe? staff? forms? honestly I’m still lost, can u give me the big picture of the whole assignment first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but giving you the big picture upfront isn't my role — that's yours to build through your own thinking, and I'm here to help with that. Actually, you just named three solid features: funding, staff, formal structures. You're not as lost as you think! So if NGOs have those features, what might that allow them to do that a looser social movement can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to explain things from scratch or give the big picture, which would undermine the learning process. I need to set a clear boundary here while acknowledging their progress. Their answers about funding, staff, and formal structures are actually quite good and I should point that out. I'll redirect them back to building on what they already know rather than giving them a top-down explanation.

exchanges.student: maybe they can do more stuff and work w governments? but then I get confused again about when NGOs are actually good or bad.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you just touched on something important. So if NGOs can work with governments and have formal structures, think about this: can you imagine a situation where that closeness to government or that formality might actually be a problem for the people they're supposed to help?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and naturally moving toward one of the core questions of the prompt - when are NGOs effective and what are their limitations. I should capitalize on this momentum. They've shown enough understanding now to start engaging with the actual prompt questions. I'll push them to think about circumstances where NGOs might be more or less effective, which is directly relevant to the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe they get too top-down or don’t listen to people? tbh I still feel like I need the full overview bc the prompt has too many parts.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation — top-down approaches and lack of local accountability are real critiques of NGOs in development literature. Notice that you just answered part of the prompt on your own. You now have thoughts on what NGOs are, what they can do, and one key limitation. Rather than seeking a full overview, why not try drafting a few sentences responding to just one part of the prompt? Which part feels most natural to start with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a full overview, which I should not provide. However, they just gave a genuinely insightful answer about NGOs being top-down and not listening to communities - this is a real limitation discussed in development literature. I should acknowledge this strongly and redirect their energy toward the prompt. We've now had several good exchanges building foundational understanding. I should point out that they actually have enough to start writing, and push them toward integrating what we've discussed rather than continuing to ask for overview explanations. This is a good moment to ask them to attempt a written response.

exchanges.student: maybe the “how good are NGOs as development agents” part, but idk how to connect it to social movements and civil society boundaries.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice to start there. The connections will become clearer once you start writing — don't worry about tying everything together yet. Try drafting 2-3 sentences on how good NGOs are as development agents, using what you've already told me. What would you write?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a good starting point and is now asking how to connect the different parts of the prompt. This is genuine intellectual progress. Rather than explaining the connections for them, I should encourage them to start writing on the part they feel comfortable with first, and trust that the connections will emerge. We've been building up to this moment and the student has enough to begin writing. I'll push them toward actually drafting something.

exchanges.student: ngl can u first just tell me how all the parts fit together? i keep starting one part then forgetting what the others want.
exchanges.tutor: I've noticed you've asked for an overview several times now, and I won't be providing one — that's not how we work together. But here's the thing: you already have the pieces. You've talked about what NGOs are, their limitations, and how social movements differ. Stop waiting for the full picture and just write those 2-3 sentences on NGO effectiveness. Once you share something written, I can help you move forward from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for an overview or full explanation multiple times in a row. This is a redundant pattern I need to address directly. I should be firm about my role and redirect them to actually attempt writing, since we've built up sufficient understanding through our conversation. The student has demonstrated they know enough to start. I need to set a clear boundary and push them to produce something written.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m still fuzzy tbh, like is the main argument basically “NGOs help but not always”?
exchanges.tutor: That's quite thin as a main argument — most readers would find it obvious. What conditions or factors would make the difference between 'helping' and 'not helping'? You mentioned top-down approaches earlier — can you build on that to make a more specific claim? Write it out and show me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still avoiding writing and looking for me to validate or provide a thesis for them. I should be firm but supportive - their proposed argument is very superficial and I should challenge them to push deeper without giving them the answer. I need to push them to actually write something rather than continuing to seek reassurance.
